data.title: USRT: Limited Upside As Valuations Stretch, Rates Stay Elevated
data.description:
Investment Thesis

iShares Core REIT ETF (USRT) continues to warrant a hold rating based on a mixed outlook for the fund. USRT offers investors a low-cost way to capture strong, diversified REITs. However, the ETF's top holdings have stretched valuations. Additionally, multiple risks exist including interest rate risk and opportunity cost compared to alternatives.

iShares Core REIT ETF - Overview and Compared ETFs

USRT is a passive ETF that tracks an index designed to represent U.S. REITs including mostly health care REITs, retail REITs, and industrial REITs. With its inception in 2007, the fund has 127 holdings and $3.77B in AUM. I wrote about the fund almost one year ago and rated the fund a hold. I believe the fund still warrants a hold for the reasons discussed in this article.

For comparison purposes, other funds examined are Schwab US REIT ETF (SCHH), Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ), and State Street Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLRE). SCHH attempts to provide investors with the total return of U.S. REITs while excluding mortgage and hybrid REITs. VNQ is the most diversified REIT fund but captures similar weights as USRT. XLRE aims to capture a representative selection of REITs within the S&P 500 Index, excluding mortgage REITs. Because all these funds compared have similar objectives, investors should look for any marginal advantages including expense ratios and dividend yield.

USRT Compared: Performance, Expense Ratio, and Dividend Yield

U.S. REIT funds have significantly underperformed the broader U.S. market over the past decade, predominantly due to inflation, interest rates, and more attractive alternatives. USRT has seen a 10-year average annual return of 5.46%, outperforming both SCHH and VNQ with 10-year average annual returns of 3.26% and 4.63%. XLRE saw the best performance with a 10-year average annual return of 6.01%.

All funds have very competitive fees and USRT has a low expense ratio of just 0.08%. A key benefit of REIT funds is their dividend yields. However, USRT currently has the lowest dividend yield of 2.65%, which has been growing at a modest rate over the past five years.

Expense Ratio, AUM, and Dividend Yield Comparison

USRT

SCHH

VNQ

XLRE

Expense Ratio

0.08%

0.07%

0.13%

0.08%

AUM

$3.77B

$9.95B

$34.19B

$7.99B

Dividend Yield TTM

2.65%

2.78%

3.61%

3.15%

Dividend Growth 5 YR CAGR

3.93%

5.54%

0.99%

Source: Seeking Alpha, 18 Apr 26

USRT Holdings and Key Outlook Factors

Due to each fund's similar objectives, top holdings are very similar. USRT's top-weighted REITs are Welltower (WELL), Prologis (PLD), Equinix (EQIX), Digital Realty Trust (DLR), and Simon Property Group (SPG). SCHH and XLRE also include American Tower Corporation (AMT). Vanguard's top holding is Vanguard Real Estate II Index Fund Institutional Plus Shares (VRTPX) which is a fund that captures many of the same REITs as VNQ itself including WELL, PLD, and EQIX.

Top 5 Holdings for USRT and Compared REIT ETFs

USRT - 127 holdings

SCHH - 121 holdings

VNQ - 145 holdings

XLRE - 31 holdings

WELL - 8.51%

WELL - 10.20%

VRTPX - 14.57%

WELL - 10.34%

PLD - 7.88%

PLD - 9.00%

WELL - 7.73%

PLD - 9.13%

EQIX - 6.69%

EQIX - 4.97%

PLD - 7.07%

EQIX - 7.27%

DLR - 4.79%

DLR - 4.62%

EQIX - 5.54%

AMT - 5.78%

SPG - 4.62%

SPG - 4.46%

AMT - 4.65%

DLR - 4.79%

Source: Multiple, compiled by author on 18 Apr 26

USRT's top three holdings make up almost a quarter of the fund's weight. As a result, these top REITs have a strong impact on the ETF's outlook. While each of these REITs has noteworthy upsides, there are distinct drawbacks discussed below.

Welltower: Pricey REIT Capitalizing on Senior Housing Supply and Demand

Welltower, USRT's #1 holding, has been benefiting from a favorable supply and demand dynamic in senior citizen housing. While the U.S. elderly population is seeing relatively high growth, new construction in senior housing is at a historic low. This lack of new supply has caused WELL to see a 3-year AFFO growth CAGR of 17.2%, driven by its over 900 senior housing communities.

Additionally, Welltower boasts a laddered debt maturity profile with a significant portion of its debt reaching maturity between 2027 and 2029. Welltower's weighted average interest has been hovering around 4%. Given that much of its debt is reaching maturity in 2027 through 2029, the REIT may also benefit from lower interest rates predicted around that period. However, Welltower is pricey after a one-year share price climb of 43%. As a result, WELL's price over forward adjusted funds from operations, or P/AFFO, is 40.5x, over double that of average REITs. Therefore, WELL will see limited upside even with increasing senior housing demand.

Prologis: Massive Power Generation Not Yet Significantly Impacting Profitability

The second key factor is Prologis, USRT's #2 holding. PLD is a logistics REIT with warehouses that support Amazon, Walmart, and other high-volume suppliers. Over the past few years, Prologis has been incorporating power generation and storage as an additional layer to its REIT portfolio. As a result, the REIT is expected to reach upwards of one gigawatt of solar and power storage across its portfolio of real estate.

While power generation is a profitable segment, Prologis's power generation is mainly geared at powering its own 5,900 properties. Therefore, while Prologis's investment in this segment may allow its real estate to be sustainable through renewable energy, its power generation does not substantially increase its funds from operations. As a result, its 3-year AFFO growth CAGR is -3.22%. The REIT has also seen a one-year share price return of 42%, bringing its forward P/AFFO to 30x. Therefore, like WELL, PLD is already priced for significant growth.

Equinix: Capturing Massive Data Center Growth, But At a Price

The third holding for USRT is Equinix, a data center REIT. EQIX has seen strong fundamentals with gross bookings, a metric for total revenue, hitting 42% year-over-year. Additionally, the demand for data centers is expected to triple by the end of the decade with $7 trillion in data centers and upgrades. Another key factor is the massive real estate demand for these data centers. Hyperscale data centers, for example, require up to 10 million square feet. Due to this demand, revenue and AFFO for EQIX have been strong and consistent.

However, EQIX has two key drawbacks that will dampen its outlook. First, while Equinix has seen increasing revenue, its capital expenditures have also been sharply rising. The REIT now has a 3.8x net leverage ratio, indicating that it is reliant on continued profitability of data centers. Second, EQIX saw a 37% one-year share price return, stretching its forward P/AFFO of 25.6x. Therefore, its debt and current valuation will weigh down EQIX from seeing meaningful upside.

Current Valuation and 2026 Outlook

REITs have seen similar fluctuations to the rest of the U.S. market due to conflict in Iran and global oil supply disruptions. The fund has since bounced back and is now within 5% of its all-time high seen in 2021. However, the fund still significantly underperformed the S&P 500 Index, which has seen a one-year return of over 34%.

Because cash flow is a more important indicator for REITs than earnings, P/CF is the most relevant valuation metric to compare REIT funds. Comparing P/CF, USRT is the most attractive ETF compared. However, this overall metric can be deceiving. USRT may have the lowest P/CF, but its top-weighted REITs are priced for perfection. Therefore, I expect USRT to continue to underperform the broader U.S. market for at least the remainder of 2026. If interest rates fall sharply, this could push USRT's outlook more towards a buy.

Valuation Metrics for USRT and Peer Competitors

USRT

SCHH

VNQ

XLRE

P/E ratio

30.86x

31.79x

30.50x

37.22x

P/B ratio

2.24x

2.68x

2.40x

2.78x

P/CF ratio

15.91x

16.33x

18.22x

18.36x

Source: Compiled by Author from Multiple Sources, 18 Apr 26

Risks to Investors

A significant risk factor for REITs is interest rates. REITs have done well in times of persistently low-interest rates. This outperformance was seen during the 2009 through 2012 period when interest rates were low. Additionally, REIT yields were comparatively higher than both bonds and savings accounts during this time. However, when rates are either elevated or rising, REITs underperform. This scenario played out over the past five years as USRT has seen a 5-year average annual return of just 5.6%, as the Federal Funds rate hit over 5% in 2023.

Looking ahead, the forecast for interest rates remains elevated. While rates may drop slightly to between 3.5% and 3.7%, they are not expected to drop much lower. Additionally, REIT yields are not substantially higher than bonds or savings accounts. USRT has only a 2.65% yield. By comparison, Vanguard's Total Bond Market ETF (BND) has a yield of 3.90%. Additionally, high-yield savings accounts have yields over 4% currently while offering lower risk. Therefore, investors seeking income will likely experience opportunity cost of investing in REIT funds.

Concluding Summary

USRT provides exposure to quality REITs in promising industries like data centers and senior healthcare. However, expected growth and demand are already priced in. Additionally, elevated interest rates will pressure these REITs that are dependent on affordable borrowing costs. Income-seeking investors will not see a significant advantage in USRT's dividend yield compared to alternatives like bonds and high-yield savings accounts. An entry point for USRT is possible if interest rates drop suddenly or valuation compression occurs.

data.title: Valley National Bancorp Has Risen Nicely And That Trend Should Continue
data.description:
Back in early December of last year, I made the decision to reaffirm Valley National Bancorp (VLY) as a 'buy' candidate. This was in spite of the fact that the stock had been underperforming the market, and it was driven by my overall view of the valuation of the company and its asset quality metrics. Since then, things have turned out nicely. While the S&P 500 is more or less flat, shares of this prospect have risen 14.2%. This is thanks to continued growth when it comes to both the income statement and the balance sheet. The company is benefiting from a declining interest rate environment, and asset quality remains robust. Despite the fact that the stock has risen, shares are still priced at levels that I would consider to be attractive enough to justify a bullish outlook, even if barely. Because of that, I will maintain my 'buy' rating on it right now.

Of course, my opinion could change as new data comes in. And it just so happens that, in the coming days, management will be announcing results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Those results will come out before the market opens on April 23rd. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue and profitability for the institution will improve nicely. So unless something big comes out of the woodwork that materially changes my view of the business, I think that sticking with my bullish outlook is warranted.

I'm Still Banking on Valley National Bancorp

Fundamentally speaking, I would say that things are going quite well for Valley National Bancorp right now. Take the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year as an example. Deposits for the institution totaled $52.18 billion. That's up solidly from the $50.08 billion that the company reported a year earlier. This overall expansion is nice to see, though it is worth noting that uninsured deposit exposure did increase somewhat. The firm went from having $12.6 billion, or about 25% of its overall deposits, classified as uninsured to having 14.6% classified under that category. That's about 28% of its overall deposits. The good news is that this is still below, even if only marginally so, the 30% maximum that I typically like to see. So that is great in and of itself.

As the value of deposits increased, the value of loans grew also. The company went from having $48.24 billion worth of loans to having $49.55 billion worth over the last year. That is positive in and of itself. However, it is worth noting that the company does have a lot of exposure when it comes to non-owner-occupied commercial real estate. About $11.57 billion, or 23.1%, of its gross loan values is dedicated to that category. Not far behind would be commercial and industrial loans at $10.96 billion. And then we have multifamily properties at $8.57 billion. Whenever I see a lot of exposure to non-owner-occupied commercial real estate, I become worried about exposure to office assets. Office vacancy rates are quite high right now, and that means additional risk for companies that have exposure to them. The good news is that only about $3 billion, or 6% of its total loan portfolio, is dedicated to these assets. That is very manageable in and of itself.

The value of securities came in at $7.78 billion at the end of 2025. That's up from $6.97 billion reported the previous year. Cash and cash equivalents, meanwhile, went from $1.89 billion down to $1.58 billion. Debt declined from $3.30 billion to $3.06 billion. What this shows is that management is restructuring the balance sheet in a way that has allowed it to focus more on higher-yielding opportunities like loans and securities while simultaneously reducing leverage using low-yielding or zero-yielding cash. The end result here is undeniably positive when it comes to profitability as well.

In the chart above, you can see the net interest margin for the company. From the final quarter of 2024 to the final quarter of 2025, the business went from 2.92% per year to 3.17%. Yes, declining interest rates have impacted it to some extent. The chart shows a decline in loans as an example. But both taxable and tax-exempt securities have started yielding it more. On top of this, as interest rates drop, the company has seen a decline in how much it is having to pay depositors. This makes perfect sense when you consider that, for the most part, deposits end up adjusting lower immediately after interest rates are slashed. And we are now, even if only barely, in a declining interest rate environment.

In the chart above, you can see the impact that the growth in the company's balance sheet, as well as the change in its composition that has aided it in boosting net interest margin, has had on net interest income. It has grown nicely year over year. However, we have also seen some improvement when it comes to non-interest income. There were multiple contributors to this, including a change in gains and losses on the sale of loans and other assets. The company also saw a nice jump in revenue coming from service charges on deposit accounts. But what really sticks out to me is the fact that capital markets income skyrocketed from $27.2 million to $42 million. The company benefited from higher volumes of interest rate swap transactions associated with commercial lending activities and a rise in loan syndication transactions. The end result of all of these improvements has been a surge in net profits from $108.7 million in the final quarter of 2024 to $188 million in the final quarter of 2025 and a rise in net profit for the year as a whole to $569 million compared to the $358.9 million reported the year before that.

Using the figures achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced at the moment. The 12.3 price-to-earnings multiple reading that we get here is a bit higher than the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. However, four of the five institutions that I compared it to in that chart are more expensive than it is. In the chart below, meanwhile, you can see that the company is actually quite cheap on a price-to-book and price-to-tangible book basis. It's actually trading at a slight discount to book value. Furthermore, only one of the five institutions that I compared it to is cheaper than it is in this regard.

I would normally be on the fence about an institution that's trading at a price-to-earnings multiple as high as what Valley National Bancorp is currently trading for. However, there are other factors that allow me to remain slightly bullish. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading for the company of 1.24%. That is above the 1% maximum that I typically like to see. What's more, four of the five banks that I compared it to ended up being worse off than it is in this regard. The chart below shows a return on equity rating of 10.12%. Only two of the companies that I compared it to are worse off here. But that is still above the 10% minimum that I like to see. The only thing I don't like is that credit quality isn't as high as I would like it to be. Take non-performing loans as a percentage of total loans as an example. Those rose from 0.74% in 2024 to 0.87% in 2025. Non-performing assets, meanwhile, increased from 0.76% to 0.87%. For context, I typically like to see a reading for both of these of no more than 0.50%.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, management will soon be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. The current expectation that analysts have is for revenue of $533.7 million. That's up from the $478.4 million reported a year earlier. Earnings per share, meanwhile, should grow from $0.18 to $0.27. That would mean a jump in net profits from $99.1 million to $152.2 million. Assuming that nothing major comes out of the woodwork, this would indicate that the stock is even cheaper on a forward basis than it is using results from last year. If we were to annualize the expectations the analysts have in terms of profitability for the first quarter, as an example, that would mean a price-to-earnings multiple that drops from the aforementioned 12.3 to 11.5. That's still higher than I would like it to be, but it's not absurd.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my belief that Valley National Bancorp is doing a really solid job. It's certainly not the cheapest prospect out there. But it is cheap enough, in my view, that investors should be slightly bullish. Based on this, I have no problem maintaining it as a 'buy' for now.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Tariff Refund Rush Begins
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Tariff refund tool will launch on April 20, US Customs says. (00:13) Walmart (WMT) tests store back rooms to speed marketplace deliveries: FT. (01:01) Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) is said to explore new AI chip partnership with Marvell (MRVL). (01:54)

This is an abridged transcript.

Importers seeking tariff refunds will be able to submit requests starting today.

The electronic application process comes two months after the Supreme Court ruled that President Trump exceeded his authority in using the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to impose sweeping tariffs on goods from almost every trading partner.

The initial phase of refunds is expected to cover roughly $127B of the ~$166B in tariffs collected.

The U.S. Customs and Border Protection has been managing the process, which includes interest payments.

The portal opens at 8am.

We’re taking a deeper dive into this topic in the Wall Street Breakfast newsletter. Here’s a link to register.

Walmart (WMT) is testing a new strategy in its competition with Amazon (AMZN).

The company is using storage space in the back rooms of stores to speed up delivery of products sold by third-party marketplace sellers, the Financial Times reported Sunday.

Walmart’s online marketplace offers roughly 500 million items from outside merchants, who keep ownership of goods until they sell and pay Walmart commissions and shipping fees.

Traditionally, these sellers store inventory at their own facilities or Walmart (WMT) warehouses. Orders shipped from Walmart (WMT) sites usually arrive within one or two days, while direct shipments from sellers can take longer.

Now Walmart (WMT) is experimenting with holding select marketplace products inside stores, allowing same-day delivery in some cases.

The pilot is underway in several Dallas-area stores, a market Walmart has often used to test new ideas.

Alphabet’s Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) is in discussions with Marvell Technology (MRVL) to develop two new chips designed to improve the efficiency of running artificial intelligence models.

According to a report Sunday from The Information, citing people familiar with the matter, one chip would be a memory processing unit intended to work alongside Google’s tensor processing unit, or TPU. The second would be a new TPU specifically engineered for AI inference workloads.

Google has been working to position its TPUs as an alternative to Nvidia’s (NVDA) graphics processing units, which currently dominate the AI chip market.

According to the report, Google and Marvell hope to complete the design of the memory processing unit as early as next year before moving it into test production.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Greg Abel puts his stamp on Berkshire Hathaway as Buffett era fades

Carney says U.S. ties are now weaknesses; Lutnick slams Canada's trade strategy

Eli Lilly nears $2B-plus deal for Kelonia to expand cancer pipeline: WSJ

Catalyst watch:

  • Tempus AI (TEM) will present research at the AACR annual meeting.

  • The three-day Adobe Summit will begin. Adobe (ADBE) is expected to unveil major product and strategy updates. Analysts think the event will serve as a window into how the company is positioning its platform against broader AI adoption trends.

Stock index futures are lower as optimism over a potential U.S.-Iran peace deal fades.

Iran will not participate in a second round of talks with the United States, according to Iranian state media, which said Tehran objected to what it described as excessive and unrealistic demands from Washington.

Crude oil is up 6% at $88. Bitcoin is up 1.7% at $75,000. Gold is down 0.7% at $4,795.

The FTSE 100 is down 0.6% and the DAX is down 1.4%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Compass Pathways (CMPS) +25% - Psychedelic-focused stocks rallied after Donald Trump signed an executive order aimed at accelerating research and expanding access to treatments for PTSD, signaling a potential regulatory and commercialization tailwind for the sector.

data.title: Dodge & Cox Stock Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Performance1

Total Returns (%)

Average Annual Total Returns Average Annual Total Returns Average Annual Total Returns Average Annual Total Returns Average Annual Total Returns
3 Months YTD 1 Year 3 Years 5 Years 10 Years 20 Years
Stock Fund — Class I -1.67 -1.67 7.93 14.02 9.66 12.72 8.81
Stock Fund — Class X -1.65 -1.65 8.04 14.12 9.75 12.76 8.83
S&P 500 Index -4.33 -4.33 17.80 18.32 12.06 14.16 10.53
Russell 1000 Value Index 2.10 2.10 15.87 14.31 9.43 10.58 8.12

Returns represent past performance and do not guarantee future results. Investment return and share price will fluctuate with market conditions, and investors may have a gain or loss when shares are sold. Mutual Fund performance changes over time and currently may be significantly lower than stated above. Performance is updated and published monthly. Current month-end performance can be obtained at dodgeandcox.com or by calling 800-621-3979.

Market Commentary

U.S. equities declined broadly in the first quarter of 2026, with the S&P 500 falling 4.3%—its worst quarterly return in over three years. The conflict in Iran disrupted global energy markets and effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz—a critical transportation corridor in the Persian Gulf for roughly 20% of global oil and natural gas supply. Oil prices rose above $100 per barrel, fueling inflation concerns. The Russell 1000 Value finished the first quarter up modestly (up 2.1%), outperforming the broader indices and the Russell 1000 Growth Index (down 9.78%).

Higher oil prices and shifting interest rate expectations pressured growth-oriented technology stocks. Investors shifted out of growth stocks amid reduced economic growth expectations. While the valuation gap between value and growth stocks narrowed, it is still relatively wide: the Russell 1000 Value trades at 16.2 times forward earnings compared to 23.5 times for its growth counterpart.2

Market concentration remains high, as the ten largest S&P 500 constituents represent approximately 40% of the S&P 500's total market capitalization. These mega-capitalization stocks, primarily in Information Technology and Communication Services, declined sharply during the quarter and had a disproportionate negative impact on the Index's returns.

Portfolio Strategy

AI continued to dominate sentiment, as investors rewarded perceived AI "winners" and penalized perceived "losers" during the quarter, creating significant valuation dislocations. We believe a value-oriented approach is especially important in the current environment. The notable recent volatility has created opportunities in select areas, including the Software industry.

In Software, we added to the Fund's position in Microsoft (MSFT).3 We believe the current valuation, at 20.0 times forward earnings, does not reflect the company's long-term growth potential and its pivotal role in the AI ecosystem. We also initiated a position in Roper Technologies (ROP), a leading software and technology company that provides critical technology solutions and systems of record for small and medium businesses. Following a recent decline in its stock price, Roper now trades at 16.1 times forward earnings—an attractive valuation given the company's importance to its customer base, strong free cash flow generation, and active share buyback program.

We also continue to evaluate holdings that have been under pressure due to AI-related concerns. One example is Booking Holdings (BKNG), a dominant global travel platform (including Booking.com, Priceline, and Kayak) with high market share in hotel and travel reservations, particularly in Europe. The company's stock price recently declined on fears that AI may replace its core service for customers. We believe Booking's infrastructure would be extremely difficult for competitors to replicate, as over 10,000 European hotels rely on Booking for visibility and online reach. We believe AI is unlikely to cause consumers to abandon Booking's platform, and investors are underestimating the durability of the company's competitive advantage. Given the lower valuation, we decided to add to the Fund's position.

Outlook

Overall, the Fund trades at an attractive valuation of 13.8 times forward earnings, a significant discount to both the S&P 500 and the Russell 1000 Value at 19.1 times and 16.2 times, respectively. The portfolio is diversified across a broad range of investment themes, which is important in this volatile environment. While narrow market leadership and sector rotations may influence short-term performance, we remain focused on our three- to five-year investment horizon and long-term fundamentals. Thank you for your continued confidence in Dodge & Cox.

Performance Review (Fund's Class I Shares vs. S&P 500)

First Quarter

Key contributors to relative results included the Fund's:

  • Information Technology underweight position, most notably Microsoft, and stock selection;
  • Industrials overweight position and stock selection, particularly FedEx (FDX) and Johnson Controls (JCI); and
  • Energy overweight position and stock selection, especially positions in Occidental Petroleum (OXY) and Baker Hughes (BKR).

Key detractors from relative results included the Fund's:

  • Financials overweight position and stock selection, notably Fidelity National Information Services (FIS), Fiserv (FISV), and Capital One (COF);
  • Consumer Staples underweight position; and
  • Position in Humana (HUM).

Performance Review (Fund's Class I Shares vs. Russell 1000 Value)

First Quarter

Key contributors to relative results included the Fund's:

  • Materials stock selection, notably LyondellBasell (LYB);
  • Industrials overweight position, particularly FedEx; and
  • Positions in Occidental Petroleum and Baker Hughes.

Key detractors from relative results included the Fund's:

  • Stock selection in Information Technology, notably Microsoft;
  • Health Care overweight position and stock selection, particularly Humana;
  • Financials holdings, notably Fidelity National Information Services and Fiserv; and
  • Position in Booking Holdings.

This agreement cannot be terminated prior to April 30, 2026, other than by resolution of the Fund's Board of Trustees. For purposes of the foregoing, ordinary expenses shall not include nonrecurring shareholder account fees, fees and expenses associated with Fund shareholder meetings, fees on portfolio transactions such as exchange fees, dividends and interest on short positions, fees and expenses of pooled investment vehicles that are held by the Fund, interest expenses and other fees and expenses related to any borrowings, taxes, brokerage fees and commissions and other costs and expenses relating to the acquisition and disposition of Fund investments, other expenditures which are capitalized in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles, and other non-routine expenses or extraordinary expenses not incurred in the ordinary course of the Fund's business, such as litigation expenses. The term of the agreement with respect to the maintenance of the net ordinary expense ratio differential between Class X and Class I shares will automatically renew for subsequent three-year terms unless terminated with at least 30 days' written notice by either party prior to the end of the then-current term. The agreement does not permit Dodge & Cox to recoup any fees waived or payments made to the Fund for a prior year.

The Fund invests in individual stocks and other securities whose market values fluctuate within a wide range, so that your investments may be worth more or less than its original cost. The Fund's performance could be hurt by equity risk, market risk, manager risk, liquidity risk, and derivatives risk. The Fund may use derivatives to create or hedge investment exposure, which may involve additional and/or greater risks than investing in securities, including more liquidity risk and the risk of a counterparty default. Some derivatives create leverage.

Before investing in any Dodge & Cox Fund, you should carefully consider the Fund's investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses. "Russell®" is/are a trade mark(s) of the relevant LSE Group companies and is used by any other LSE Group company under license.

  1. All returns are stated in U.S. dollars, unless otherwise noted. The Fund's total returns include the reinvestment of dividend and capital gain distributions, but have not been adjusted for any income taxes payable by shareholders on these distributions or on Fund share redemptions. Index returns include dividend and/or interest income but, unlike Fund returns, do not reflect fees or expenses. The Class X shares inception date is May 2, 2022. The returns shown prior to that date are for the Class I shares.
  2. Unless otherwise specified, all weightings and characteristics are as of March 31, 2026.
  3. The use of specific examples does not imply that they are more or less attractive investments than the portfolio's other holdings.

See Disclosures for a full list of financial terms and Index definitions.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fastenal Company: Tariff Concerns On Top Of An Expensive Valuation
data.description:
Summary

I reiterated a hold rating to Fastenal Company (FAST) earlier this year because of concerns surrounding weak pricing and slowing FMI deployment. For this update, I still think a hold rating makes sense. Demand looks better, with strong and broad-based growth across categories and geographies, and the P&L still showed operating leverage. The problem is that pricing has weakened further, and the latest Section 232 tariff changes could make margin pressure persist for longer than expected. The valuation is also still not at a cheap enough level to justify a buy yet.

Earnings results update

FAST reported 1Q26 results last week. Total revenue grew 12.4% y/y to $2.2 billion, with growth being broad-based across fasteners, safety, and other industrial products. Margins were rather weaker this quarter. Gross margin was down 47 bps y/y to 44.6%, mainly due to unfavorable price/cost, transportation headwinds, and certain customer rebates. FAST did see some operating leverage as SG&A came down 70 bps y/y to 24.3% of revenue. In terms of absolute profit dollars, EBIT was up 13.6% y/y to $447.6 million, with EBIT margin up 23 bps to 20.3%. This led to net income growth of 13.8% y/y to $340 million, and EPS growing to $0.30 from $0.26 in Q1 2025. Altogether, this was not a bad quarter in absolute terms. Revenue growth was good, category growth was broad, and profits still grew at a double-digit rate. The problem is that the margin side has turned weak.

What got better

With more than just one good month of performance (January previously), a solid revenue growth performance this quarter, and March still going strong, the obvious positive takeaway here is that the demand picture looks much better now than it did in January. Specifically, March average daily sales were up 11.5% y/y, and growth was broad-based, with Fastener sales up 14.4% y/y, Safety was up 11.3%, and other industrial products grew 11%. Every reported geography's growth was positive, too. You put everything together, and I think it is clear that demand remains healthy.

Importantly, with Fasteners being the strongest category, it makes the underlying demand look even better because Fasteners are a better reflection of core industrial activity (you buy more when times are good and less when times are bad), while Safety and other industrial products are less cyclical. As such, if the more economically sensitive product line is still leading, I'd say it makes a better demand signal. It also helps because Fasteners are the higher gross margin category. In other words, my view is that this quarter looks more like a real improvement in activity and not just a temporary bounce.

The other side of the coin

The main reason I would still stay neutral is pricing. In my previous write-up, pricing fatigue had already started to show up. Q4 2025 pricing added ~310 to 340 bps to growth, but price/cost still turned slightly negative, and there was slower-than-expected implementation of price increases, especially in non-fastener categories. In Q1 2026, it got worse. The price/cost situation has turned for the worse to ~-50 bps y/y in Q1, and I think this is a major red flag. Delayed price realization has now shown up repeatedly (management noted slow price execution), and the latest April 2026 Section 232 action makes that setup more difficult. For context, starting April 6, Section 232 applies to the full customs value of covered aluminum, steel, and copper articles and derivatives regardless of metal content. It also kept many aluminum and steel articles and certain derivative products at 50%, left another set of derivative items at 25%, and allowed additional derivative products to be added on a rolling basis.

My view is that this increases the risk of more cost pressure moving through the supply chain, and that could force FAST to keep raising prices to maintain margin at a time when price realization is already proving difficult. There is really not much FAST can do about this tariff situation in the near term, and assuming the same price/cost trend, I'd say the margin situation is very likely to get worse before we see any signs of normalization.

With that said, I do think there is an important counterbalance point to note. If you look at the P&L, even with the gross margin pressure, the EBIT margin still expanded by 20 bps y/y to 20.3%, and EBIT still printed double-digit growth of 13.6% y/y. Net income and EPS also grew at a double-digit rate. So, yes, gross margin was disappointing, but operating leverage is evident. If FAST can grow volumes fast enough, we could continue to see double-digit EPS growth even with the weak pricing realization trend. I am on the fence for now because the new Section 232 tariff action has not really shown its impact yet, so it is hard to extrapolate this quarter's operating leverage forward.

Valuation

My view on valuation has shifted for the better. Previously, my view was that extrapolating 1 good month was not sensible, especially given the pricing trends. However, FAST has shown us that growth has continued to be strong, and it was broad-based. Management also sounded much more positive on the broader market conditions today. The question now is whether FAST can grow its top line fast enough, so much that it can offset the pricing weakness and print solid earnings growth given the operating leverage it is seeing. My sense is that FAST may be able to achieve the latter, but what pulls me back from upgrading to a buy is that valuation remains expensive. Although the multiple has come down from 38.7x forward P/E to ~36x today, it is still well above the historical average of 28x. Moreover, peers such as W.W. Grainger, Inc. (GWW) and Applied Industrial Technologies, Inc. (AIT) trade at ~26x forward P/E. While it's fair that their revenue growth profile is low (high-single-digits) vs. FAST's low-teens, I would say the ~10x premium fairly reflects this faster growth.

Conclusion

My view remains neutral on FAST. I acknowledge that the demand side got better, and that makes the top-line story more credible. What still holds me back is pricing. The price/cost dynamic worsened again, and the new Section 232 tariff action raises the risk that margin pressure lasts longer than the market expects. FAST is clearly not in bad shape, and operating leverage is helping. But until I see cleaner price realization or a better valuation cushion, I do not think this is the right time to get more bullish.

data.title: Ametek: A Very Well-Run Business Is Valued Accordingly
data.description:
Last summer, I believed that AMETEK, Inc. (AME) was continuing with M&A to complement modest organic growth. The high-quality and niche industrial player announced the acquisition of FARO Technologies, which is a much larger and somewhat unusual deal for Ametek.

Ever since, shares have risen by a third, vastly outperforming the wider market, as a premium player is awarded more premium valuations here.

Trading at a 30 times earnings multiple, or 3% and change earnings yield, does not compete with risk-free rates. However, the long-term track record of compounded earnings growth is what counts, making shares not necessarily overextended, although valued at too much of a premium to see imminent appeal.

Other interesting IPOs are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

Powering On

Recently, investors in Ametek have seen quite some good news. In February, Ametek announced a 10% increase in the quarterly dividend, now paid at $0.34 per share, for an annual payout of $1.36 per share.

This hike followed the announcement of a bolt-on deal of LKC in February, used for effective diagnosis and management of ophthalmic conditions, yet unfortunately, no financial details have been announced.

Reported sales rose by nearly 7% to $7.40 billion, as fourth-quarter sales rose by 13% to $2.0 billion, as the growth acceleration was driven by the purchase of Faro. Electronic Instruments sales rose by nearly 6% to $4.92 billion, with Electromechanical sales up nearly 9% to $2.48 billion.

The company grew operating margins by similar percentages, with net earnings up just over 7% to $1.48 billion. Such earnings growth and minimal share buybacks made GAAP earnings rise some forty-seven cents to $6.42 per share. Adjusted earnings rose by sixty cents to $7.43 per share, with the majority of the reconciliation driven by amortization charges, as these numbers look quite fair.

Net debt is reported at $1.82 billion, trailing adjusted operating income, with leverage ratios seen below 1 times here, in fact, at 0.8 times, despite a $920 million deal for Faro. Aided by the deal, sales growth is seen in the mid-single to high-single digits, with adjusted earnings seen up between 6-9% to $7.87-$8.07 per share.

Steady Performance, Demanding Valuation

An $180 stock in the summer has seen strong momentum, having risen to the $240 mark in early March. Shares have seen a pullback to the lower $200s in recent weeks, but a relief rally has sent shares back up to $236 per share. This values the business at 30 times adjusted earnings here, and while leverage is minimal, these are demanding valuations, even with organic sales increases reported at 5%.

The same goes for the dividend. A 10% increase in the dividend sounds compelling and enticing, yet in the end, investors now receive just a 0.6% yield here. A near 30 times multiple is too steep with earnings yields lagging compared to treasury yields (by a wide margin), yet quality is what Ametek is. On top of the organic growth, it really is the M&A playbook, which is really the strong point of Ametek in my view.

This strategy, of acquiring promising businesses with lower margins to integrate them into the Ametek framework and generate premium margins over time as well, is the stronghold of the business. Moreover, with electronics and electromechanical being the core expertise of this business, there is some exposure to data centers here, although it seems relatively low and is not broken out separately. The same goes for defense markets, a growing yet non-quantified business as well, with Ametek's subsidiary providing batteries for AUVs, for instance.

Steady Operator

Over the past decade, Ametek has doubled sales here, which, combined with operating margin gains, has made it so that net earnings have tripled, all amidst essentially a flat share count, while the balance sheet remains strong.

The issue that I have is that shares look overextended, trading around 30 times this year's earnings, which is a demanding multiple. However, a great track record over time and more financial room for M&A provide a potential impetus for the business and investors, yet I am naturally cautious to get involved here.

However, Ametek fully deserves the premium valuation as a result of sound operating performance, including strong organic sales growth, margin expansion, and great capabilities to identify and integrate deals, all creating a balanced approach to create long-term value.

Such a track record has stood at the basis of the giant success, this being just a $3 stock in the year 2000, with shares having increased by a factor of 80 times ever since. This reveals that long-term performance counts more than current multiples, yet right now it is difficult to justify paying this near-term premium.

Shares of Ametek have seen volatility over time as well, including extended periods of stagnation, as opportunities at more modest premiums provide excellent long-term opportunities to get involved.

data.title: Becton, Dickinson: Value Or Overvalued Even Now?
data.description:
I cover quite a few healthcare and healthtech/medtech companies. I like investing in the cheap, qualitative ones that have an appealing market share and a solid moat. Becton, Dickinson (BDX) may be such a company, though it's fair to say the market is torn on the issue. Half of the market views the company as a "HOLD" - the other half, mainly Wall Street, views it as a buy. Issues specifically seem to be centered around recent results, recent changes to estimates and forecasts for the company, and the way that growth is expected to run as of 2026-2029. This seems to be the catalyst for a significant share price drop that began in February 2026.

Now, this is not the first share price drop the company has seen in the last 24 months. Looking at the share price graph, it's fair to say that the company has been through a few significant declines as a result of earnings news. Of course, the main reason for the company's significant share price drop (at least the initial one) is related to the company's spin-off. This is the Waters Corporation, which saw BD's Diagnostics and bioscience segment spun off/merged in February, about 2 months ago at the time of writing this article.

It was an RMT - a reverse Morris trust transaction, which I've covered in other articles (specifically on Unilever (UL)) to create more of a pure-play life science business, leaving BD to focus on its core medtech business. In trying to combine the best of both worlds, BD shareholders were left with just south of a 40% ownership stake in the new business.

I have 3 goals in this article. First, establish BD's bona fides by analyzing its current operations and future prospects. Secondly, the merger/spin-off - good idea, or bad, according to me? Third, the company's value and valuation. What's it worth, and at what share price do I believe the company to be a good investment?

With that in mind, let's get going.

Becton, Dickinson - Attractive fundamentals from attractive medtech

BD, without its life science arm, which we'll take a look at going forward, sells medical devices and related products. Their primary indirect focus is on improving drug delivery and enhancing the segments wherein it's active. Some of its "basic" products include commoditized supplies like syringes, catheters, and the like - but there's also a higher-value segment with better margins.

Examples of relatively simple products include things like the standard hypodermic needles, disposal systems for needles and dangerous items, as well as simple IV setups and vacuum-sealed tubes for the collection of blood.

Complex products, which the company also focuses on, meanwhile, include things like the Pyxis system (medication management cabinets with automation), the Alaris pumps (precise medication delivery, smart tech), FACSLyrics products (cell analysis systems), and many others. I'm drawn naturally to the more complex products due to higher margins and better moats. But I've also learned never to underestimate a scaled, simple product manufacturer with a solid moat.

It is fair to say that BD does a bit of everything - even after the spin-off of bioscience. It's segmented into the business areas of Medical and Interventional. Its key focal areas are, as you can probably tell from the examples above, things like drug delivery, infection control, and overall hospital/clinic safety. Good areas to be in, because they are based on repeat business, they're continually necessary, they never go away, and rather are increasing in complexity.

Viewing the company's filings, we find that the company generates about $21B of revenues, taking this through a nice 46%+ gross margin, going to 16.3% operating margin (improved recently from sub-15%), coming to about 8% net margins, which comes to about $1.7-$2B per year of net income. The company has an attractive history of generating roughly $2-3B worth of free cash flow. It has about $18.7B of net debt, and it's BBB rated. Neither the best nor the worst rating around. The company's current dividend yield, as of its recent drop, is about 2.65%. Again, neither the best nor the worst. What the company has going for it is a relatively consistent EPS growth rate of almost 9% per year for the past 20 years. What it also has going for it is a significant premiumization history since about 2011, which as of 2025, is broken. This implies, at least theoretically, we're getting the company "cheap" - which is obviously a positive as well - more on that in valuation.

To put it clearly, what we have here is a fundamentally attractive company, and the quality has been proven over time for several years.

Moving on to the recent merger - good or bad idea?

Whenever a company spins off or sells revenue-generating segments, I'm always a bit skeptical. I'm sure that management has an excellent rationale for their decision - this also is clearly communicated in the company's pre-merger considerations.

Basically, the rationale is that the segment was a better fit for Waters than it was for BD and that the new company has better potential for growth and organic expansion than it would have as a part of BD.

I have some issues with the decision, though. The company got rid of one of the higher-margin businesses that it had. In fact, the business was partly responsible for the solid operating margin expansion that we spoke about. It's not as though we're talking about a bad piece of the company. The segment also played a role in the diversification of income and revenue, which, if you follow my work, you know that I am a big fan of. Then the entire process was obviously a cost pressure - operational issues were apparent at the time, since the company tried to doubly manage a merger and its usual operations.

But the biggest issue is really that I view it as a reactive rather than a proactive decision. The so-called clarity and debt reduction that was provided could have easily been delivered by the company's own earnings over time, using exactly the high-margin income that the company has now gotten rid of. It was basically the company wanting to do something quickly rather than waiting for it. What's worse, by getting rid of the segment, the company lost bioscience complementary assets, like microbiology and sample prep - these segments had, in some cases, direct correlation to the company's own operating segments - directly or indirectly. It's, after all, why BD had them in the first place.

What is hard to argue against is the company's current position. We're talking about a company that, certainly, has grown a lot smaller than it used to be in the past. This reduced scale and size is not an advantage for BD.

I believe the net result is lower revenue, lower margins, lower profits, and lower competitive positioning in negotiation for pricing - with its old products, it was able to offer complementary services, which it now no longer can.

It's my stance that the reactiveness was caused by the significant dip about a year ago in 2025 and the before underperformance that had hounded the company since about late 2023. But in my view, this underperformance or "stale" return, was not in any way due to mistakes made by the company, simply due to a too high valuation to begin with.

Let me show you what I mean.

Becton, Dickinson Valuation - Uncertain future

For the longest time, BD was trading at a significant premium - we're talking well above 20x P/E, which was stretching it given its low dividend, relatively high debt, and industry trends, causing volatility from COVID-19 (much like other players in the sector, not in any way unique to BD).

I have been uninterested in the company for its transition that it has been doing for some time, though I looked at it in the middle of last year. Now, with the spin-off finished, I'm ready to give my initial verdict.

While life science tools are more volatile, they also carry higher margins. The company expects 25% full-year 2026E margins; the 1Q26 results, however, reported only 21.2%, which starts showing some of the impacts - even if meaningful impacts also included things like tariffs and investments. But it's still a meaningful variance from the target. I would estimate closer to a 22-23% margin over time, and perhaps a 1% reduction in net margin as well. Other analyst forecasts mirror my own expectations. Over the next 3 years, the average is expected to move from 8-9%, to about 7-8% growth, about 1% less earnings growth per year. Furthermore, the dividend growth rate is not expected to be massive either, currently estimated at less than 2 cents growth from the current level in 2027E, and an actual decline to $3.82/share based on 8 analyst estimates for 2028E.

In short, I don't consider historical premiumization, currently implying a 19-22x P/E, to be valid.

So at what rate should BDX be discounted, and at what P/E?

My estimate of a lower P/E is about skepticism of the same rates of future growth. To my mind, a 2-3% yielding company growing only at 5-7% should only sometimes be above 15-16x P/E, with 15-16x P/E being the sort of "standard" for a company. This is standard because at a 15x P/E, you're estimating about 6.67% earnings yield with "moderate" growth expectations. It means paying $15 for $1, meaning that for $1 of earnings per share. It's also the historical average for the market.

Putting it another way, is Becton, Dickinson justified in trading significantly above 15x P/E?

I say no. I would in fact, to stay conservative, put the company at this multiple, because in doing so you're taking a very conservative view of the business, relative to its 19-20x P/E 5-year average.

The good news is that even at 15x P/E as a forward multiple, you're finding a market-beating rate of return. At anything lower than $160/share, you're currently getting (yes, even with a dividend cut estimated in 2028), an annualized rate of return above 15%. This is based on an implied fair value of about $215/share for the year 2028E. Therefore, you don't even need the company to move back to its previous heights to outperform here. It shows you just what the power of "value" can be, if this makes sense. Remember, back in 2023, the company traded at over $220/share, and that was with an EPS less than 60% of the one estimated for 2028E.

I therefore view this risk as well-adjusted, and put my initial PT for the company at $160/share, with a rating of "BUY", if one its necessarily the best on the market.

Thesis

Becton, Dickinson & Company is a solid company in the medtech field. Even after what I consider to be a relatively unattractive spin-off and merger, they retain core operations with good profitability and generation of cash flow. Based on this, I consider the company a good "BUY" at a conservative entry. For the past 3 years, the company has normalized from 20-22x P/E to now trading at 13x. Based on the company's forward estimates, I consider 15x P/E to be an excellent estimate for the company, and set a "BUY" under $160/share, which currently provides a market-beating upside to the investment.

I therefore consider it a "BUY" here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • The company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically is enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $77/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "BUY" rating as of April 2026.

data.title: Table Of The Day: After S&P 500 Sees Near-Record Swing, What's Next?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Think the stock market just went from downright depressed to extremely euphoric? It’s not in your head! The S&P 500 Index (SPX) just swung from oversold to overbought in the second-shortest time frame ever.

Check out the MoneyShow Table of the Day - courtesy of Bloomberg. As you can see, the 14-day Relative Strength Index (RSI) on the S&P just surged from below 30 to above 70 in only 11 days. The index has only done that faster once in three quarters of a century worth of record-keeping. That was in August 1982, when it took six days.

In fact, since the turn of the century, there have only been four times we even came close. They were in 2016 (13 days), 2023 (15 days), 2019 (21 days), and 2014 (22 days).

So, if you’re suffering from a case of market whiplash, take heart. You’re not alone. We really have swung to giddiness in a short period of time.

Then there’s the huge shift in sector leadership. Through midday Friday, the State Street Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) was the big April winner at +14.8%. The State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE)? It was down 6.7% month-to-date.

What’s more, we have tons of high-momentum stocks leading the charge during the rebound. Space stocks? Check. Chip stocks? Check. A failing sneaker company that decided to pivot to AI? Check (for a while, anyway).

I’ve said for some time I expected H1 2026 to be volatile. And volatile it has been. But while I remain “cautiously optimistic” about markets longer term, we’re wildly overextended in the short term. So, if you’re an active trader, it might be time to cash in some chips.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: The Market's All-Time High Is A Warning Sign
data.description:
If you have been riding the stock market higher, it might not feel like we are nearing a recession. Even though I have what I would consider to be a very defensive portfolio, things have been going exceptionally well for me. I have two major portfolios and some smaller ones. Over the last year, my largest one is up 78.8%. The smaller of the two happens to be up 27.7%. When you add the two together, I am currently up 56.1% over the last year. The S&P 500 is up 34.8% over the same window of time, which is still exceptional in and of itself. I would argue, however, that this puts us at risk of meaningful downside. As I have been arguing since August of last year, the US is likely to enter into a recession before the end of 2026. And right now, the market is priced at levels that, quite honestly, are absurd. I would urge investors to tread cautiously here. And even though I am still 100% long except for having sold a few call options on one particular holding, I believe that the market could be due for some pain in the not-too-distant future. Because of this, I am bearish about the market in general right now.

Mr. Market’s exuberance

I don't think very many people will disagree with me when I say that the market is objectively expensive. On April 17th, the S&P 500 closed for the first time ever above 7,100. This is a monumental achievement and likely has many market participants expecting further upside from here. However, from a valuation standpoint, things are looking incredibly pricey. Take the chart above as an example. In it, you can see two different measures of how expensive the market currently is. The most straightforward of these is the S&P 500’s price-to-earnings multiple. This just takes the market value of the companies in the S&P 500 divided by the earnings that they generated. And then we have what is called the Shiller P/E ratio, which is a measure of how expensive the S&P 500 is that was arrived at through certain adjustments.

First and foremost, it is worth noting that this chart looks at data on an annual basis, with the only exception being the data point for April 16th of this year. That shows the current multiple of the market. So if you were to look at this monthly or quarterly, you probably would find short windows of time that look a bit different from this. But I digress. For those not familiar, the Shiller P/E is also known as the CAPE (Cyclically Adjusted P/E). While the normal price-to-earnings multiple uses the price today of the market and the earnings today, the Shiller P/E looks at the average of the last 10 years of earnings and adjusts those for inflation. The idea here is that since earnings are cyclical, a boom could make earnings look artificially high, while recessions could make them look incredibly low. So by employing this alternative measure, you are essentially smoothing earnings across what is believed to be a full economic cycle.

What I find to be really fascinating here is that it doesn't matter which of these price-to-earnings multiples we use. In the chart above, you can see, from the time that the data was traced back to 1918, the 10 windows of time during which the S&P 500 was at its most expensive. All of these represent a full year, with the exception being the April 16th data for 2026. What I find to be really uncomforting about this measure of the S&P 500 is the fact that every single window of time here, except for here, the last two years that look at how expensive the market was on that date, and except for April 16th of this year, represented periods that were either around the Dot-Com Bubble, the lead-up to the housing collapse of 2008/2009, or the COVID-19 pandemic. Of these 10, five windows of time were ones in which the market was more expensive than it is now. This means that, throughout all of recorded history dating back to 1918, the market has only been more expensive in relation to earnings in five separate years. And in each of those cases, it was around a bubble or following the rise of a pandemic when corporate earnings plunged more than share prices did.

To be honest with you, the picture is not any more comforting when you look at it through the lens of the Shiller P/E ratio. In fact, only three windows of time happen to be more expensive than the market is right this moment. And one of those was the window of time ending in January of this year. And in this case, all of these windows of time fell under either the dot-com bubble or the COVID-19 pandemic, except for the window of time ending in January 2018. So yes, it would be accurate to say that the market is historically expensive.

I know that some investors might argue that it is expensive because of certain catalysts that should propel growth higher. The biggest thing that comes to mind here would be AI. But as I detailed in a previous article, we do seem to be in bubble territory when it comes to AI specifically. We aren't really seeing the kinds of productivity improvements economy-wide that would justify the multiples that we are experiencing at this moment. It is important to note that corporate earnings have indeed been on the rise. In the chart above, you can see precisely what I mean here. But all of this occurred after the COVID-19 pandemic. Corporate price gouging, inflationary pressures, a rapid shift for some industries to focus more of their efforts on the digital economy, increased spending caused by significant government stimulus, and other factors can be blamed or thanked, depending on your position, for this increase in corporate profits.

The problem, however, is that when you dig deeper, that narrative starts to show cracks. Yes, corporate profits have never been higher. But at the same time, the jobs market is showing significant weakness. I won't rehash all the data that I have dug into in previous articles. But in the chart above, you can see the trailing 12-month job gains that the US has seen since January of 2025. Although we did see a strong month in March, with 152,000 jobs added, the overall trend here has been decidedly negative. However, the data doesn't just stop there.

In the chart above, you can see year-over-year changes in real personal income. Ever since around the end of the first quarter of 2025, we have seen a collapse in year-over-year improvements. We aren't yet at the point of contraction. But we do seem to be heading that way. In February, for instance, the year-over-year increase was only $182. That was a gain of only 0.9%. For context, a year earlier, the gain was $450, or 2.3%. This helps to explain why consumer confidence levels continue to worsen, as the chart below illustrates. Even though consumers are still experiencing inflation-adjusted wage increases, those are becoming increasingly smaller. And when you combine, moreover, the fact that we are still dealing with a cost-of-living crisis in this nation, it makes sense that consumers would be unhappy with what is going on.

There are broader economic indicators that show that the picture is worsening. If you follow the economy closely, you already know that we are dealing with a decline in investment in residential properties. I have covered that in other articles, and I don't feel pressed to do that here. In the chart below, you can see private fixed investment in non-residential structures for the manufacturing industry as an example. This shows contraction as well. Year over year, private fixed investment was down 11.7%. This counters the narrative that tariffs and trade deals are resulting in increased investment in the US. If anything, weakening economic conditions are offsetting that, and more so. Of course, we do need to put this all in proper context. Because of significant government stimulus, after the COVID-19 pandemic, major investments in economic growth opportunities were made. That's likely what caused at least some of the inflationary pressures that we saw. So we are still above what we have been in the past. Substantially so, in fact. So some of this drop is likely the result of industry conditions normalizing. But that doesn't change the fact that a decline is a decline all the same, which is the opposite of what we would want if the market were doing well.

I could point to multiple other economic indicators to make my point. Almost all of them look disappointing at best and horrible at worst. But instead of looking at each of these in turn, perhaps the best thing we can do is look at the Coincident Economic Activity Index. This is an index that tracks what is currently happening in the economy. It looks at multiple items such as employment data, real personal income data, industrial production data, and real manufacturing and trade sales data. Even though the chart below makes it seem like we have just reached back to normal levels, when you drill in deeper, you find something that is worrisome. The reading that we got most recently, which covers December of 2025, is the lowest reading that we have seen since March of 2021. And if we ignore the window of time that constituted the COVID-19 pandemic, you would have to go back to September of 2010 to see a reading lower than what we currently have. This does not mean that the economy is shrinking. But it does mean that it is certainly slowing.

I am not the only one who is worried about matters right now. Earlier this month, the Federal Reserve came out with its minutes from its meeting in March. In those minutes, they said that the economy is currently expanding at a solid pace. But some recent growth in GDP that was demonstrated came from temporary factors like the unwinding of the government shutdown. The Federal Reserve believes that economic growth is not as strong as it believed it to be previously. And they expressed worry over the fact that job gains remain low and that risks to employment are skewed to the downside. The labor market appears to be particularly vulnerable to adverse shocks. Because of low hiring activity, a decline in demand could push the unemployment rate up rather quickly.

On top of this, we are continuing to see inflation that is above what the Federal Reserve is targeting for the long haul. And this was even before the impact of higher oil prices caused by the war against Iran. Tariffs have been a key problem when it comes to inflationary pressures. And the risk that inflation will grow from here is rising. When you add all of this together, it looks as though broader economic uncertainty is expanding. And when you factor into all of this the fact that credit is ‘somewhat restrictive’ for households, small businesses, and commercial real estate, and that borrowing costs are high while delinquency rates have been rising, there is a lot to be worried about.

Takeaway

An expensive market is a problem when it occurs at a time when the economy is getting worse. All that indicates is that the potential for downside is significant. After all, if market participants come to realize just how weak the economy is, and if that weakening worsens, there could be selling that meaningfully outpaces buying at current levels. The end result would be a collapse in the market. Now, unless there is some sort of contagion risk bubbling up somewhere, such as what could be the case in the private credit market or through overly aggressive investments in AI, I don't necessarily think that this downturn will be like what we saw during the 2008 and 2009 financial collapse.

That could happen, but it's not my base scenario. Rather, a 10% to 15% drop absent a recession looks highly likely. And if economic conditions do deteriorate as I expect them to, I wouldn't be surprised to see markets fall 20% or more. Of course, in the meantime, I will continue to buy undervalued prospects. My investment strategy is not immune to market volatility. But the types of companies that I buy into should theoretically hold up better if I turn out to be right. So I don't have any intention to turn to bearish strategies. But I am becoming increasingly bearish about where the market is right now.

data.title: S&P 500 Earnings: Forward Estimated Growth Is Really Remarkable, Revenue Growth Too
data.description:
Originally published on April 19, 2026

When the news gets too good in the stock market, it can be as much of a warning sign as an incentive for either equity allocations to float higher in client accounts to take advantage of bullish economic and corporate conditions or trim positions and raise a little cash.

Here’s a good post from Ryan Detrick of Carson Group that shows the S&P 500’s 7% jump in 7 days and then “expected forward returns”.

The S&P 500 is back to overbought already, but fundamentals remain pretty solid - as the rest of this blog details (supporting the technical analysis).

Each week, this blog updates S&P 500 forward EPS estimates using LSEG data, which allows me to look at revisions to forward estimates and see which sectors might be a safe place to allocate client funds or trim some gains from a particular sector.

Usually at this time of the quarter, with Q1 ’26 earnings just starting, S&P 500 EPS watchers are seeing slightly negative or downward revisions to Q2, Q3, and Q4 numbers, as the tendency is to be cautious about the forward quarter’s as current results - in this case Q1 ’26 - reflect that conservatism and show an “upside surprise” to estimates. Particularly with Iran and its headlines, I thought some weakness would occur in the forward estimates, and so far that’s not been the case.

Q1 and Q2 ’26 expected S&P 500 EPS growth is still relatively flat with a slight positive bias, but look at Q3 ’26 expected EPS growth revision to +22.4% today, versus the +14.4% on January 2nd, ’26. Tech peaked in late October ’25, but I can’t recall any particular damage to any sector that would have caused downward revisions to any particular sector or sectors in Q3 ’25 that would have resulted in unusually easy compares for the S&P 500.

So, what is the market discounting in Q3 ’26, or are these just “bad numbers” from the Street?

To try and get some educated estimate of what’s happening, let’s look at expected S&P 500 revenue growth (in the above table, look at the last four columns, which show expected S&P 500 revenue growth by quarter in 2026):

Note the 200 bp acceleration for Q1 and Q2 ’26, which is a pretty healthy revision, since revenue “upside surprise” factors are typically 1-2.0%. Note also that expected revenue growth for the S&P 500 in each quarter in 2026 is higher than the actual average revenue growth per quarter in 2025.

2025 actual revenue growth by the S&P 500 “averaged” 7.2%. Here’s the progression by quarter in 2025 in terms of actual revenue growth:

  • Q4 ’25: +9.2%
  • Q3 ’25: +8.3%
  • Q2 ’25: +6.4%
  • Q1 ’25: +5.0%

What might be even more interesting is that looking at actual revenue growth for the S&P 500 by quarter since Q4 ’12 (the first quarter this blog began tracking quarterly “actual” S&P 500 EPS and revenue data), S&P 500 revenue growth “averaged” +3.2% from Q4 ’12 to Q4 ’19. The strongest revenue quarters within that time period happened with the “Tax Cuts And Jobs Act” passed in Q4 ’17. Here’s how those quarters tracked in terms of revenue growth:

  • Q3 ’18: +8.6%
  • Q2 ’18: +8.5%
  • Q1 ’18: +8.4%
  • Q4 ’17: +8.3%

Because these revenue growth rates are “post TCJA”, and then after 2018 the revenue growth rate slows again into 2019 (and then we have the Covid mess), it’s easier to conclude that what we are seeing today in the S&P 500 is probably considerably aided by the “One Big Beautiful Bill” (OBBB) even in the face of tariffs of 2025, and now the crude oil shock of 2026.

The tech sector is expecting 27.1% revenue growth in Q1 ’26, along with expected 46% EPS growth.

To answer the above question about Q3 ’26 and potential bad numbers, Q3 ’26 might be lapping peak OBBB or the 2025 tariffs, it might be an implied forecast for crude oil and having it come down to the pre-Iran level of $67 per barrel (WTI).

S&P 500 data

  • The forward 4-quarter estimate (FFQE) for the S&P 500 rose this past week to $340.96 from $339.22 last week.
  • The S&P 500 P/E on the forward estimate rose to 20.9x this week.
  • The S&P 500 “earnings yield” (SP EY) fell to 4.78% this past week on the sharp bounce in the S&P 500. The +5% EY yield in late March ’26 was a good tell for stocks, but I prefer not to make predictions around metrics such as these.
  • While just 48 companies have reported their Q1 ’26 financial results so far, the EPS upside surprise is still quite healthy at 8.8%, while the S&P 500 revenue upside surprise is also healthy at +2.2%. Another 94 companies are expected to report this week, per the LSEG report “This Week in Earnings”.

Summary

Having tracked a lot of this data (from LSEG) from post-2008 until today, the remarkable aspect of this current period is the lack of any negativity or discouragement from analysts in terms of seeing quarterly forward numbers taken down, as is usually the pattern.

Financials had a very good quarter last week. Some Citi (C) was added after their earnings release, while this blog owns enough of JPM already.

This week, earnings previews will be written on IBM and BA, two turnaround stocks. BA is struggling as they near peak revenue in 2018, and yet, their margins and operating income and EPS are nowhere near records, which tells me that the FAA restriction on deliveries every quarter is keeping BA from leveraging the manufacturing base - but that what’s got them into trouble to begin with.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All EPS and revenue data is sourced from LSEG. Readers and clients should monitor their own comfort with portfolio volatility and adjust accordingly.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
